explanation: The requirement is that if the system's power has been off for more than 15 minutes, then it should not be possible to select "Resume Ventilation." Mapping this to the atomic propositions, "off" means the system is completely de-energized/off, and "resumeVentilation" indicates selection of Resume Ventilation. To encode the "off for more than 15 minutes" condition, we need to assert that after the system has been in "off" state for longer than the threshold (here, set as N_DURATION=15), "resumeVentilation" must not occur ever after. This corresponds to a safety property: after being off for the given interval, "resumeVentilation" is always disallowed. Template index 8 encodes this by stating that globally, it is always not the case that both the relevant condition (off for >15 min) and "resumeVentilation" is true. In this simple case, we interpret the requirement as: It is always not allowed to "resumeVentilation" if the system has been off for more than 15 minutes.

The requirement is: once the system power has been off for more than 15 minutes, it must not be possible to select 'Resume Ventilation'. We have 'off' (system is off) and 'resumeVentilation' (user attempts to resume ventilation). The correct LTL pattern is to forbid 'resumeVentilation' after 'off' has been true for a stage exceeding 15 minutes. The most direct temporal specification is: once there is a stretch of 'off' for longer than 15 time units, 'resumeVentilation' is globally forbidden thereafter. This fits template 8, which globally forbids the event, augmented by template 3 semantics to encode the 'after N minutes condition' via a hold. However, with only boolean expressions as parameters, the atomic proposition to indicate 'off' has held for more than 15 minutes must be modeled as a new proposition (not present); lacking it, we can use: If at any time, 'off' has held continuously for more than 15 minutes, then from that point on, 'resumeVentilation' is forbidden. This fits LTL template 157: '((! (off & (X (!off)))) U ((off & (X (!off))) & (X (G (! resumeVentilation))))) | (G (! (off & (X (!off)))))', where (off & (X (!off))) marks the instant 'off' ends after a contiguous span; the preceding U captures time; changing the time to 15 units would require 'off' holding for 16 steps, invariant to the templates. This captures the requirement. Set N_DURATION = 15.

The property specifies that whenever the system has been off (atomic proposition: 'off') for longer than 15 minutes, it must always be the case that the 'Resume Ventilation' option cannot be selected (atomic proposition: 'resumeVentilation' is false). We use the bounded globally operator 'G[0,N_DURATION]' with N_DURATION=16 to overapproximate 'more than 15 minutes' using discrete time steps. Thus, 'G (!resumeVentilation)' captures that it is always not possible to select 'Resume Ventilation' as soon as the off state extends beyond the bounded interval. This matches the requirement in the natural language statement.

The natural language requirement specifies that if the power of the system has been off for more than 15 minutes, it should not be possible to select “Resume Ventilation”. In the atomic propositions, 'off' indicates the system is currently completely de-energized and off, and 'resumeVentilation' indicates that 'Resume Ventilation' is selected. The correct LTL encoding is: Globally, if the system has been off for 16 or more consecutive steps (assuming 1 step = 1 minute, so N_DURATION = 15), it should always be the case that 'resumeVentilation' does not occur. That is: for any time interval of at least 16 consecutive steps where 'off' holds, 'resumeVentilation' must never become true. This fits the template for: whenever a condition holds globally over a duration, a second property never holds (template index 8). Therefore, we encode: G((G[0,15] off) -> G(!resumeVentilation)), which, using the nearest template, is simplified to say: 'Globally, it is never possible to select resume ventilation if the system has been off for the specified duration.'

The requirement is: once the power is off for more than 15 minutes, selecting "Resume Ventilation" should not be possible. We map 'off' to indicate the power being off, and 'resumeVentilation' to the action of selecting "Resume Ventilation". The behavior needs to express that after the system has been off for more than a certain amount of time (here, 15 minutes, which must be mapped to a time-bounded LTL operator), it is always the case that 'resumeVentilation' does not become true. This fits template 8: 'G (! bool_exp3)', which ensures that globally, it is never possible to resume ventilation. Since we want this only after the system has been off more than 15 minutes, we must express the state where the system has been continuously off for 15 minutes, after which 'resumeVentilation' must always be false. The atomic propositions do not provide a direct timer, but the best approximation within the propositional context is to globally prohibit 'resumeVentilation' once 'off' has persisted for 15 minutes. For actual systems, additional temporal tracking logic may be needed, but using available atomic props, 'G (off -> G (! resumeVentilation))' would be desired, but since the template bank supports only atomic propositions in the designated positions, the closest correct mapping is: globally, it is not possible to resume ventilation, after off is true for a continuous period N_DURATION (here, set to 15), i.e., after F[0,15](off) then G(!resumeVentilation). However, with available templates, 'G (!resumeVentilation)' (template 8) is most faithful, with the implicit system design enforcing the time condition outside the LTL (e.g., via a mode switch).

The natural language states that if the system has been 'off' for more than 15 minutes, it should not be possible to select "Resume Ventilation". In LTL, this means that always, if the system is off continuously for more than 15 time-units (assuming each step is one minute), then eventually (in the next state after this duration and onward) it must always be the case that 'resumeVentilation' does not hold (i.e., cannot be selected). The best fitting template is to require that after the system has been continuously off for N_DURATION steps (here, 15), globally, it is not possible to select resumeVentilation; in LTL: G((G[0,15] off) | G off) -> G(!resumeVentilation). In our case, the suitable template concisely expresses this ban after extended off-period, so we use: ((G[0,N_DURATION] (!bool_exp3)) | (G (!bool_exp3))) & (F[0,N_DURATION+1] bool_exp3) interpreted as 'after being off for 15 steps, eventually resumeVentilation does NOT become true' and ban resumeVentilation after that. Thus, bool_exp3 = off & resumeVentilation, N_DURATION=15.

The requirement is: after the system has been off for more than 15 minutes, 'Resume Ventilation' cannot be selected. Since only boolean expressions (not temporal ones like G[0,15] off) can appear in the template boolean expression slots, we must restructure the property. The best approximation is to express: If the system is off NOW and was also off in the PREVIOUS step, then 'resumeVentilation' cannot be selected in the current (or next) step. Since the templates and boolean expressions do not differentiate duration without temporal operators, we must choose a trigger based solely on the 'off' atomic proposition. The best, sound approximation is to state: if 'off' holds, then 'resumeVentilation' cannot be selected (i.e., while the system is off, 'Resume Ventilation' is impossible). While this does not capture the exact duration, it is the tightest we can express without temporal formulas in the trigger. Template 98, 'G (bool_exp1 -> (! bool_exp3))', fits this as it expresses that globally, whenever the system is 'off', 'resumeVentilation' cannot be selected.

The requirement states that once the system has been off (de-energized) for more than 15 minutes, it should not be possible to select 'Resume Ventilation'. Here, 'off' is the atomic proposition indicating the system is completely de-energized and off, and 'resumeVentilation' indicates the action to select Resume Ventilation. We capture the fact that after the 'off' state holds for a duration longer than 15 minutes, 'resumeVentilation' must remain prohibited. Assuming 15 minutes corresponds to a certain number of time steps (let's fix N_DURATION=15 for this mapping), the best way is to state that after a contiguous period of 'off' for 15+ steps, 'resumeVentilation' is globally not possible. LTL pattern 8 (G (!bool_exp3)) fits: it globally forbids 'resumeVentilation' after a specific state. As the condition is triggered by 'off' persisting for 15 steps, we use (G[0,15] off) as the trigger, and the property holds globally after this point. Thus, we choose template 8, and set bool_exp3 = resumeVentilation, so that 'resumeVentilation' is always forbidden.

The requirement stipulates that once the system power has been off for more than 15 minutes, it must never be possible to select 'Resume Ventilation'. That is, after a contiguous period of at least 15 minutes (which we represent as N_DURATION=15, with each time step 1 minute) where 'off' is true, it must always be the case from that point on that 'resumeVentilation' is not true. This is a classic pattern expressible using template 8: 'G (! bool_exp3)', i.e., 'Globally, resumeVentilation is not possible' if the system has been off for more than 15 minutes at any prior point. To encode this using the available templates and boolean atoms, the viable structure is to say: "Globally, if off has JUST transitioned to true and has remained true for 15 or more steps, then globally resumeVentilation is not possible." The best approximation is to use template 8 ('G (! resumeVentilation)') with a guard to ensure this only activates after the off period, which matches closest to the logic needed from available templates.

However, since there are no templates allowing direct reference to counting time or to past time modalities, and only the 'off' and 'resumeVentilation' atomic propositions are involved, the best fit is to continuously forbid 'resumeVentilation' after the 'off' condition, which is what template 18 ('G (bool_exp2 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))') provides. Here, bool_exp2 can capture 'off has held for N_DURATION', and bool_exp3 is 'resumeVentilation'. But as you noted, 'G[0,15] off' is not a boolean expression, so instead, we use a flag variable that detects when the 'off' condition has been held for N_DURATION, say 'off_15min', and then write 'G (off_15min -> (G (! resumeVentilation)))'. Since such a flag does not exist in the provided atomic propositions, the closest feasible expression is:

'G ((off_last_15min) -> G (! resumeVentilation))', which cannot be fully expressed with the given atoms alone. Therefore, the next best encoding is:

'G (off -> (G (! resumeVentilation)))' (template 18): Whenever the system is off, it must be the case that resumeVentilation cannot be selected.

Of note, this is more restrictive than the original requirement (forbids resumeVentilation whenever the system is off, rather than once it has been off for 15 mins), but with only atomic 'off' and 'resumeVentilation' at hand, it is the closest representable property in the LTL format from the provided templates and atoms.

The requirement is: Once the system has been off for more than 15 minutes, it should never be possible to select 'Resume Ventilation'. Using the atomic propositions, 'off' can represent the system being off, and 'resumeVentilation' can represent selecting 'Resume Ventilation'. We encode 'the system has been off for more than 15 minutes' as 16 consecutive time steps (N_DURATION = 16), assuming 1 minute per step for this abstraction. The property should state that after 'off' is true for 16 steps, 'resumeVentilation' must never happen. We use the following template: (G (((! off) & (X off)) -> (X (G (! resumeVentilation))))) & (off -> (G (! resumeVentilation))). This means: whenever we transition from not-off to off, starting from that point, after the off period extends 16 steps (more than 15), then resumeVentilation must never occur. However, the cleanest LTL pattern is 'if off holds for 16 or more steps, then G (! resumeVentilation)'. Template 8 fits: G (! resumeVentilation), (but only after the duration), so instead we use template 3: enforces (!resumeVentilation) for [0,16], and eventually allows resumeVentilation. But that doesn't capture the 'never after duration'. The best is: after 16 steps of off, G(!resumeVentilation). For this, we use 'G ((off & G[0,16] off) -> G (!resumeVentilation))', but this is not in the list. So, we select: template 18: G (off -> (G (! resumeVentilation))). This will enforce: as long as 'off' holds, resumeVentilation is not possible (but does not enforce the 'after 16 min' detail). Given template limitations, this is closest with the provided options.
